title: Chow rings and modified diagonals
speaker: Kieran O'Grady
affiliation: Sapienza - Università di Roma; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Beauville and Voisin proved that decomposable cycles (intersections of divisors) on a projective K3 surface span a 1-dimensional subspace of the (infinite-dimensional) group of 0-cycles modulo rational equivalence. I will address the following question: what is the rank of the group of decomposable 0-cycles of a smooth projective variety? Beauville and Voisin also proved a refinement of the result mentioned above, namely a decomposition (modulo rational equivalence) of the small diagonal in the cube of a K3.

proof_stages.label: Beauville-Voisin distinguished zero-cycle on a K3 surface
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the Beauville-Voisin theorem for projective K3 surfaces, defining the distinguished degree-one zero-cycle via points on rational curves and proving that intersections of two divisors are multiples of it.

proof_stages.label: Rank problem for decomposable zero-cycles and the large-rank theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker asks how large the group generated by intersections of divisors can be and states a theorem producing very general high-degree surfaces in P3 through prescribed disjoint curves for which H^2 and the curve self-intersections are linearly independent in CH0.

proof_stages.label: Ambient relations and the nonambient-canonical hypothesis
proof_stages.summary: The necessary relations among H^2, C·H, and C^2 are computed by push-pull from P3 and by adjunction, explaining why the hypothesis that KC is not induced from the ambient projective space is needed.

proof_stages.label: Universal blow-up family and spreading of relations
proof_stages.summary: After blowing up P3 along the prescribed curves, the universal family of surfaces in the relevant linear system is constructed, vertical codimension-two cycles are defined, and the spreading principle is used to translate a very-general fiberwise quadratic relation into a vertical class upstairs.

proof_stages.label: Vertical classes and Noether-Lefschetz elimination
proof_stages.summary: Using the projective-bundle description of the Chow group, the kernel condition supplied by the canonical-class hypothesis, special vertical classes from surfaces singular along the curves, and a Noether-Lefschetz statement, the proof rules out all relations except the known ones.

proof_stages.label: Modified diagonals and the K3 reformulation
proof_stages.summary: Modified diagonals Γm(X,a) are defined on powers of a smooth projective variety, and Beauville-Voisin's K3 theorem is reformulated as the vanishing of the third modified diagonal, which implies both the divisor-intersection and c2 statements.

proof_stages.label: Implication to decomposable cycles and failure of the converse
proof_stages.summary: The vanishing of Γn+1(X,a) for an n-dimensional variety is shown to force every product of n divisors to be a multiple of a, but the previously constructed surfaces and a countability argument show that this divisor-product property does not imply modified-diagonal vanishing.

proof_stages.label: Hyperkähler modified-diagonal conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk ends with the conjecture that every hyperkähler variety of dimension 2n has a degree-one zero-cycle whose (2n+1)-st modified diagonal vanishes, recovering the Beauville-Voisin result in dimension two.

examples.name: Blow-ups at very general points
examples.note: Blowing up a regular surface at n very general points gives independent exceptional self-intersections, illustrating that decomposable zero-cycles can have arbitrarily large rank in an artificial way.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs blow-ups at n very general points and uses Mumford plus exceptional self-intersections to argue the resulting decomposable zero cycles are independent

passes.reason: Ordinary blow-ups at very general points are used to show decomposable zero-cycle rank can be arbitrarily large, defeating any general rank-one or boundedness expectation.

Blowing up a regular surface at very general points is an ordinary construction used to show the rank of decomposable zero-cycles can be arbitrarily large, so no uniform/rank-one expectation can hold.

Used to show decomposable zero-cycle rank can be made arbitrarily large by blowing up very general points, refuting any small/uniform-rank expectation; the blow-ups themselves are ordinary members of a standard construction.
An ordinary generic blow-up construction used to witness arbitrarily large decomposable rank, not a structurally special surface.
Generic blow-ups are used to show decomposable zero-cycle rank can be arbitrarily large, refuting rank-one or bounded expectations.
ordinary blow-ups at generic points are used to show decomposable zero-cycle rank can be arbitrarily large, so no rank-one or bounded analogue can hold generally
Used to show the rank-one/bounded decomposable-cycle behavior fails in general by producing arbitrarily many independent exceptional self-intersections.

Blowing up n very general points gives n independent exceptional self-intersection zero-cycles, showing decomposable cycles can have arbitrarily large rank, while the surfaces are non-sporadic generic blow-ups.
used as an artificial construction showing decomposable rank can be arbitrarily large, while a blow-up at very general points is not intrinsically symmetric, sporadic, degenerate, or extremal
Used to show decomposable zero-cycle rank can be made arbitrarily large by blow-up exceptional intersections, while the blow-ups at very general points are not structurally sporadic or extremal.

Used to show the Beauville--Voisin rank-one behavior is not general, since blow-ups at very general points give arbitrarily many independent decomposable zero-cycles, but the blow-ups are not structurally exceptional.

Used as a standard construction showing decomposable zero-cycles can have arbitrarily large rank, but a blow-up at very general points is not intrinsically sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.

examples.name: Lifou's degree-eight surface
examples.note: A known degree-eight surface in projective 3-space with decomposable zero-cycles of rank at least two is cited as a less artificial motivating instance.
examples.engagement_reason: Lifou’s degree-eight surface is only cited as an existing surface whose decomposable cycles have rank at least two

passes.reason: Lifou's smooth degree-eight surface is a non-sporadic hypersurface example with decomposable cycles of rank at least two, used against extending the K3 rank-one phenomenon to smooth surfaces in P3.

Lifou's smooth degree-eight surface is not a sporadic or structurally extreme surface, and is cited because its decomposable zero-cycles have rank at least two, defeating the rank-one Beauville–Voisin-type behavior in this setting.

Lifou's degree-eight surface is cited as a smooth surface in P3 with decomposable cycles of rank at least two, showing the K3 rank-one behavior need not persist; it is not structurally sporadic or extremal in the classification sense.
A smooth degree-eight surface is structurally ordinary, but Lifou's example is invoked to show the rank-one decomposable-cycle phenomenon fails for some surfaces in P3.
Lifou's smooth degree-eight surface is cited as a hypersurface with decomposable cycles of rank at least two, so it witnesses failure of rank-one behavior.
Lifou's smooth degree-eight surface is a non-sporadic surface used to show the decomposable-cycle rank can exceed one even in a natural projective class
Used as a smooth degree-eight surface in P3 whose decomposable zero-cycles have rank at least two, refuting a naive rank-one extension.

Lifou's smooth degree-eight surface has decomposable-cycle rank at least two, contradicting any rank-one Beauville-Voisin-type expectation for smooth surfaces in P3, but is not a classification-exceptional or extremal object.
cited as a natural smooth surface in P3 with decomposable cycles of rank at least two, not as a classification-exception or extremal surface
Used as a smooth degree-eight surface whose decomposable zero cycles have rank at least two, refuting a naive rank-one extension of the K3 phenomenon.

Used to show that even smooth degree-eight surfaces in projective three-space can have decomposable zero-cycle rank at least two, while the surface is not a sporadic or extremal classification object.

Lifou’s degree-eight surface is invoked to show the rank-one decomposable-cycle phenomenon fails for smooth surfaces in P3, while the surface is not a classification exception or structurally extreme.

examples.name: Surfaces with Picard group generated by lines
examples.note: These surfaces show that large Picard rank alone does not force large decomposable-cycle rank, motivating the extra hypothesis excluding curves whose canonical class is induced from the ambient projective space.
examples.engagement_reason: surfaces with Picard group generated by lines are invoked as a contrasting class, with only a brief qualitative explanation about intersections behaving like lines

passes.reason: Surfaces whose Picard group is generated by lines are ordinary special surfaces used to show that large Picard rank alone need not give a large decomposable-cycle group.

Surfaces whose Picard group is generated by lines are structurally just special Noether–Lefschetz-type surfaces, but they are used to show that large Picard rank alone is not enough to force many independent decomposable zero-cycles.

Surfaces whose Picard group is generated by lines are used to show that large Picard rank alone is insufficient for large decomposable-cycle rank; this is a special but systematic family, not degenerate, extremal, exceptional, or maximally symmetric.
Surfaces whose Picard group is generated by lines are ordinary Noether-Lefschetz-type examples used to show large Picard rank alone does not force large decomposable rank.
Surfaces with Picard group generated by lines are invoked to show that large Picard rank alone does not force large decomposable-cycle rank.
surfaces with Picard group generated by lines are ordinary special surfaces used to refute the idea that large Picard rank alone forces large decomposable-cycle rank
Used to show that large Picard rank alone is not enough, since line-generated Picard groups can still force the decomposable intersections to collapse to the expected small span.

Surfaces whose Picard group is generated by lines are used to show that large Picard rank alone does not force many independent decomposable zero-cycles, and they form a systematic class rather than a degenerate, extremal, exceptional, or maximally symmetric one.
used to show large Picard rank alone does not force large decomposable-cycle rank, but such surfaces are not structurally exceptional in the strict sense
Used to show large Picard rank alone is insufficient, since Picard groups generated by lines still give only the expected line-type intersection relations.
Used as an obstruction to the naive idea that large Picard rank suffices, since Picard groups generated by lines can still yield only the line-like decomposable relations.

Surfaces whose Picard group is generated by lines are used to show that large Picard rank alone does not force large rank of decomposable zero-cycles, but this line-generated Picard structure is not exceptional, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.
